quiz.q: How many main body segments does a honey bee have?
quiz.why: Like all insects, bees have three segments: head, thorax, and abdomen.

quiz.q: Which honey bee develops from an UNfertilized egg?
quiz.why: Drones are haploid — they hatch from unfertilized eggs and have only the mother's genes.

quiz.q: Honey bees are haplodiploid. This means drones are…
quiz.why: Drones are haploid; females (workers and queens) are diploid.

quiz.q: What is 'bee space'?
quiz.why: 3/8" is the gap bees leave open. Smaller, they fill it with propolis; larger, they build burr comb.

quiz.q: A queen mates with an average of about how many drones?
quiz.why: Queens mate with ~12 drones (range 10–20) on 1–3 mating flights, boosting genetic diversity.

quiz.q: Where are the glands that produce royal jelly located?
opts: In the heads of nurse bees
quiz.why: Hypopharyngeal glands in nurse bees' heads secrete royal jelly to feed larvae.

quiz.q: Which organ stores sperm in the queen after mating?
quiz.why: The spermatheca stores sperm for the queen's whole life, releasing it to fertilize eggs.

quiz.q: Worker bees secrete wax from glands located on the…
quiz.why: Wax glands sit on the underside of the worker's abdomen, most active before she becomes a forager.

quiz.q: On average, honey bees spend how long as larvae?
quiz.why: All castes average ~6 days in the larval stage before pupating.

quiz.q: Pollen is the colony's primary source of…
quiz.why: Pollen (as bee bread) supplies protein, fats, vitamins and minerals; nectar supplies carbohydrate.

quiz.q: Nectar is the colony's main source of…
quiz.why: Bees convert carbohydrate-rich nectar into honey for energy.

quiz.q: A drone congregation area is where…
opts: Drones gather in the air to mate with virgin queens
quiz.why: Virgin queens fly to these areas to mate with many drones.

quiz.q: Which female bee has a fully developed reproductive system?
quiz.why: The queen is the only fully reproductive female; workers' ovaries are greatly reduced.

quiz.q: Only which bees can sting?
quiz.why: The stinger is a modified ovipositor (egg-layer), so only females have one.

quiz.q: What does the waggle dance communicate?
opts: Direction and distance to a resource
quiz.why: The waggle's angle gives direction and its duration gives distance to food or a nest site.

quiz.q: Below about what temperature do bees form a winter cluster?
quiz.why: Around 64°F bees cluster to stay warm; foraging and water collection stop.

quiz.q: Heater bees keep the brood nest at roughly…
quiz.why: Brood must be incubated at 91–97°F (33–36°C) to develop properly.

quiz.q: 'Festooning' (bees hanging in chains by their legs) is associated with…
quiz.why: Festooning bees are usually building comb.

quiz.q: Robbing is when honey bees…
quiz.why: Robbing is bees taking stores from another colony instead of foraging flowers.

quiz.q: An orientation flight is taken to…
opts: Learn the hive's location and surroundings
quiz.why: Young bees take short orientation flights to memorize the hive location, also needed after a move.

quiz.q: A cleansing flight is when bees leave the hive to…
quiz.why: Bees hold waste through winter and void it on warm days — fecal spots on snow can be a good sign.

quiz.q: The tremble dance recruits bees to…
quiz.why: When a forager can't find a receiver bee, she tremble-dances to recruit more nectar handlers.

quiz.q: Bees can navigate using polarized light because they…
opts: Can see it even through clouds
quiz.why: Bees detect polarized sunlight to orient while foraging, even on cloudy days.

quiz.q: Heavy orange/brown streaking on the front of a hive most likely signals…
quiz.why: A little fecal matter is normal; heavy streaking is a warning sign.

quiz.q: Many queen cells along the BOTTOM edge of frames usually indicate…
quiz.why: Numerous cells low on the comb are swarm cells — the colony is preparing to split.

quiz.q: A few queen cells near the CENTER of the comb usually indicate…
quiz.why: Central cells usually mean the workers are replacing a failing or lost queen.

quiz.q: Supersedure is…
opts: Workers replacing an old or failing queen
quiz.why: In supersedure the colony raises a new queen to replace the existing one, often without swarming.

quiz.q: A laying worker's eggs develop into…
quiz.why: Laying workers' eggs are unfertilized, so they become drones.

quiz.q: Seeing multiple eggs in single cells most often means…
quiz.why: A good queen lays one egg per cell; multiple eggs per cell point to laying workers.

quiz.q: Emergency queen cells are built when…
opts: The colony suddenly loses its queen
quiz.why: Workers convert young worker larvae into queens after an abrupt queen loss.

quiz.q: A 'queenright' colony is one that…
quiz.why: Queenright simply means a laying queen is present.

quiz.q: In the Doolittle method of queen rearing, larvae are…
quiz.why: Young larvae are grafted into cups and given to cell-builder colonies to raise as queens.

quiz.q: How many mating flights does a virgin queen typically take?
quiz.why: A queen takes 1–3 mating flights to a drone congregation area.

quiz.q: Roughly what percentage of adult bees are drones at peak (late spring/early summer)?
quiz.why: ~5% at peak. Far more capped drone late summer can signal a drone-laying queen.

quiz.q: A nuc (nucleus colony) is…
opts: A small colony of about 2–5 frames
quiz.why: Nucs are small colonies made by pulling frames and bees from larger ones.

quiz.q: The 'mating sign' a returning queen may carry is…
opts: Part of the drone's endophallus left behind
quiz.why: When a drone mates, his endophallus ruptures; the tip may remain in the queen.

quiz.q: American foulbrood (AFB) is caused by a…
quiz.why: AFB is caused by the spore-forming bacterium Paenibacillus larvae and is highly infectious.

quiz.q: The 'ropy test' (twisting a toothpick in a cell) checks for…
quiz.why: If the larval remains rope out 2 cm+, suspect AFB.

quiz.q: Which disease leaves a hard, dark scale that will NOT scrape out of the cell?
quiz.why: AFB scale sticks tightly to the cell wall — a key field clue.

quiz.q: Chalkbrood is caused by a…
quiz.why: The fungus Ascosphaera apis turns larvae into hard chalky 'mummies.'

quiz.q: European foulbrood (EFB) is caused by a…
quiz.why: EFB is caused by Melissococcus plutonius; larvae usually die before capping.

quiz.q: Nosema is a disease of the bee's…
quiz.why: Nosema is a gut fungus; N. apis is linked with dysentery.

quiz.q: Deformed Wing Virus is spread mainly by…
quiz.why: Varroa vectors DWV — crumpled wings are a red flag for high mite loads.

quiz.q: Sacbrood is caused by a…
quiz.why: Sacbrood virus leaves fluid-filled, sac-like larvae that turn brown.

quiz.q: Wax moths are mainly a problem in…
quiz.why: Wax moths exploit weak colonies and unprotected stored comb.

quiz.q: Which problem is the most serious and often requires burning equipment?
quiz.why: AFB spores survive decades; burning is often the only sure control.

quiz.q: Where can U.S. beekeepers send samples for FREE disease diagnosis?
quiz.why: The USDA Beltsville Bee Lab in Maryland offers free diagnosis.

quiz.q: Tracheal mites live and feed inside the bee's…
quiz.why: Acarapis woodi infests the tracheae, feeding on hemolymph.

quiz.q: Small white 'guanine' crystals on the ceilings of brood cells indicate…
quiz.why: Guanine deposits are mite waste — a sign of heavy Varroa.

quiz.q: Dysentery (brown streaking inside/outside the hive) is most associated with…
quiz.why: Nosema apis in particular is linked with dysentery.

quiz.q: Chalkbrood 'mummies' look like…
opts: Hard, chalky white or black larvae
quiz.why: Affected larvae dry into chalk-like mummies that may rattle in cells or appear at the entrance.

quiz.q: A small, fast black beetle that slimes the comb in warm, humid conditions is the…
quiz.why: Small hive beetle larvae ferment and ruin comb and honey, worst in heat and humidity.

quiz.q: Varroa mites reproduce…
quiz.why: A foundress mite enters a cell before capping and raises offspring on the developing pupa.

quiz.q: Varroa prefer to reproduce in which type of brood?
quiz.why: Drone brood's longer development lets mites raise more mature daughters.

quiz.q: The 'phoretic' (dispersal) phase of Varroa is when mites are…
quiz.why: Phoretic mites travel on adult bees between brood cells and colonies.

quiz.q: A reliable way to monitor Varroa levels is…
opts: An alcohol wash or sugar roll
quiz.why: Washing a half-cup of bees gives an actual mite count; eyeballing bees does not.

quiz.q: Which Varroa treatment's active ingredient is amitraz?
quiz.why: Apivar uses amitraz; supers must be off and you wait 2 weeks after.

quiz.q: Which treatment uses formic acid and can be used with honey supers on?
quiz.why: MAQS (formic acid) can stay on during a 7-day treatment, but needs moderate temps.

quiz.q: Oxalic acid works best when the colony is…
quiz.why: Oxalic doesn't penetrate capped brood, so it's most effective when little/no brood is present.

quiz.q: Which two products are NOT recommended due to resistance and wax residues?
quiz.why: Both persist in wax for years and mites have widespread resistance.

quiz.q: Thymol-based treatments like Apiguard need what conditions to work well?
quiz.why: Thymol must vaporize, so it needs warmth (roughly 60–105°F).

quiz.q: Why should you rotate Varroa treatments?
opts: To slow the development of resistance
quiz.why: Rotating chemistries and following IPM reduces the chance mites become resistant.

quiz.q: At minimum, how many brood frames should you inspect per box?
quiz.why: Inspecting at least 4 brood frames gives a fair picture without over-disturbing the nest.

quiz.q: A healthy brood frame looks like a…
opts: Rainbow — brood center, pollen arc, honey on top
quiz.why: Brood in the center, ringed by pollen, then honey, with only 6–17% empty cells.

quiz.q: Each year you should aim to replace how many of the oldest frames per box?
quiz.why: Rotating out the 2 oldest frames yearly removes built-up pathogens and pesticides.

quiz.q: You should never remove more than how many frames from a hive at once?
quiz.why: Pulling more than 4 at once diverts the colony into comb-building instead of brood/honey.

quiz.q: A 1:1 sugar syrup (equal sugar and water) is mainly used to…
quiz.why: Thin 1:1 syrup mimics a nectar flow and stimulates the queen to lay.

quiz.q: A 2:1 sugar syrup (twice as much sugar) is mainly used to…
opts: Build stores for winter in fall
quiz.why: Thick 2:1 syrup helps colonies pack away winter stores.

quiz.q: Scratch marks at the hive entrance most likely mean…
quiz.why: Skunks scratch to lure bees out and eat them; raising the hive on a stand helps.

quiz.q: When examining a brood frame, you should hold it with…
opts: The sun at your back, light shining into the cells
quiz.why: Backlighting the cells reveals eggs, larvae and the brood pattern.

quiz.q: Integrated Pest Management (IPM) means you treat when pests…
quiz.why: IPM uses monitoring and treats based on thresholds, reducing unnecessary chemical use.

quiz.q: Putting frames back in the same order and orientation helps…
quiz.why: The nest is organized; scrambling frames disrupts brood and stores.

quiz.q: Bee bread is…
quiz.why: Pollen packed with honey and microbes ferments into bee bread, a key protein source.

quiz.q: A 'dearth' is a period when…
opts: Little nectar or pollen is available
quiz.why: During a dearth, forage is scarce and robbing risk rises.

quiz.q: Abundant white royal jelly pooled around young larvae indicates…
quiz.why: Plenty of jelly means nurses have enough protein; thin/no jelly suggests feeding is needed.

quiz.q: A pollen SUBSTITUTE differs from a supplement because it…
quiz.why: Substitutes (soy, yeast, milk powder) have no pollen; supplements mix in some real pollen.

quiz.q: Ideal pollen/bee-bread stores for brood rearing are about…
opts: Only what fits in one cell
quiz.why: Roughly 1–2 frames of pollen per brood box supports healthy brood rearing.

quiz.q: Seeing 10–20 bees per minute entering with full pollen baskets indicates…
quiz.why: Steady incoming pollen is a sign lots of resources are coming into the colony.

quiz.q: Bees cap honey when its moisture is about…
quiz.why: At ~17–19% water the honey is ripe and the bees cap it.

quiz.q: Honey is at risk of fermenting when moisture rises above about…
quiz.why: Above ~18% moisture, wild yeasts can ferment honey.

quiz.q: Crystallization in honey is the formation of…
quiz.why: Glucose naturally crystallizes; it doesn't mean the honey is spoiled.

quiz.q: Propolis is made by bees from…
quiz.why: Foragers gather plant resins and mix them with wax to seal and protect the nest.

quiz.q: A hive 'stocking rate' refers to…
opts: Hives needed per area of a crop for pollination
quiz.why: Stocking rate is how many colonies an acre/crop needs for adequate pollination.

quiz.q: A pollination contract is an agreement between…
quiz.why: It's a binding contract for renting colonies to pollinate a crop.

quiz.q: Mead is made by fermenting…
quiz.why: Mead is an alcoholic drink fermented from honey and water.

quiz.q: Ultrafiltered honey (every particle removed) is legally considered…
quiz.why: The FDA classes ultrafiltered product as a sweetener because pollen and all particles are gone.

quiz.q: How many eyes does a honey bee have?
opts: Five — two compound and three simple (ocelli)
quiz.why: Two large compound eyes plus three small ocelli that sense light intensity.

quiz.q: The three small simple eyes on top of a bee's head are called…
quiz.why: Ocelli detect light intensity but can't form an image.

quiz.q: How many wings does a honey bee have?
quiz.why: Two pairs; hooks called hamuli link the front and hind wing on each side in flight.

quiz.q: A bee's main organs of smell are its…
quiz.why: The antennae carry most of the bee's smell and touch receptors.

quiz.q: Bees breathe through small openings along the body called…
quiz.why: Air enters through spiracles and travels via tracheae to the tissues.

quiz.q: The honey bee equivalent of blood is called…
quiz.why: Hemolymph bathes the organs directly rather than flowing through veins.

quiz.q: Bees draw up nectar and water using their…
quiz.why: The proboscis is a tube the bee forms to suck up liquids.

quiz.q: The honey bee organs that act like kidneys are the…
quiz.why: Malpighian tubules filter waste from the hemolymph into the hindgut.

quiz.q: Which bee has no stinger at all?
quiz.why: Drones are male; the stinger is a modified egg-layer found only in females.

quiz.q: A summer worker bee typically lives about…
quiz.why: Summer workers wear out in roughly 6 weeks; winter bees live for months.

quiz.q: Winter bees survive for months partly because they have…
opts: Larger fat bodies and more vitellogenin
quiz.why: Winter bees are physiologically distinct, with large fat bodies for longevity.

quiz.q: Vitellogenin in bees is important for…
quiz.why: Vitellogenin supports egg-making, royal jelly, immune function and long life.

quiz.q: The fat body of a bee mainly functions to…
quiz.why: The fat body stores fat/glycogen and makes key proteins.

quiz.q: The valve that keeps nectar in the honey crop from being digested is the…
quiz.why: The proventriculus closes off the crop from the ventriculus (stomach).

quiz.q: How many legs does a honey bee have?
quiz.why: All insects have three pairs of legs.

quiz.q: Pollen is carried back to the hive packed into the…
opts: Pollen baskets (corbiculae) on the hind legs
quiz.why: Foragers pack pollen pellets into the corbiculae on their hind legs.

quiz.q: From egg to adult, a QUEEN develops in about…
quiz.why: Queens are fastest at ~16 days; workers ~21; drones ~24.

quiz.q: From egg to adult, a WORKER develops in about…
quiz.why: Workers take about 21 days from egg to emergence.

quiz.q: From egg to adult, a DRONE develops in about…
quiz.why: Drones take the longest, about 24 days.

quiz.q: A honey bee egg hatches into a larva after about…
quiz.why: Eggs of all castes hatch around day 3.

quiz.q: Developing queens are fed royal jelly…
quiz.why: Queens get royal jelly throughout; workers only the first 2–3 days, then bee bread and honey.

quiz.q: The order of honey bee metamorphosis is…
quiz.why: Bees undergo complete metamorphosis through a distinct pupal stage.

quiz.q: A peanut-shaped cell hanging downward off the comb is a…
quiz.why: Queen cells are large, vertical and peanut-textured.

quiz.q: Workers sense their queen is present and healthy mainly through her…
quiz.why: Queen pheromone spreads through the colony; its decline signals queenlessness.

quiz.q: The alarm pheromone, which smells like bananas, is released when a bee…
quiz.why: Alarm pheromone near the sting recruits other bees to defend.

quiz.q: Bees stand at the entrance and fan to release Nasonov pheromone in order to…
opts: Help orient and recruit nestmates (mark home/water)
quiz.why: Nasonov pheromone marks the hive entrance, water, and swarm clusters.

quiz.q: Brood pheromone, released by larvae, helps…
quiz.why: Larval brood pheromone influences foraging and suppresses worker ovaries.

quiz.q: Bees clustering OUTSIDE the entrance on a hot evening ('bearding') are usually…
opts: Cooling off and ventilating the hive
quiz.why: Bearding is normal heat behavior, not necessarily swarming.

quiz.q: Fanning at the entrance with the abdomen down often means bees are…
opts: Cooling the hive or curing honey
quiz.why: Wing-fanning moves air to lower temperature and evaporate nectar moisture.

quiz.q: 'Absconding' is when…
opts: The entire colony abandons the hive
quiz.why: Absconding is the whole colony leaving, often due to pests, disease or disturbance.

quiz.q: Undertaker bees are workers that…
opts: Remove dead bodies and debris from the hive
quiz.why: A subset of middle-aged bees keeps the nest clean by removing corpses.

quiz.q: Drifting is when bees…
opts: Enter the wrong hive by mistake
quiz.why: Drift is common when hives look alike and sit close together.

quiz.q: In late fall, colonies evict their drones because…
opts: Drones don't work and would drain winter stores
quiz.why: Drones are costly to feed and useless in winter, so workers force them out.

quiz.q: A virgin queen flies to a drone congregation area to mate because it…
quiz.why: Mating away from home with many drones boosts genetic diversity and avoids inbreeding.

quiz.q: Polyandry (a queen mating with many drones) benefits the colony by…
opts: Increasing genetic diversity and disease resistance
quiz.why: Diverse genetics helps the colony resist disease and adapt.

quiz.q: Instrumental (artificial) insemination of queens is used to…
opts: Control exactly which drones a queen mates with
quiz.why: It gives breeders full control over genetics, unlike open mating.

quiz.q: A two-queen colony is sometimes run to…
quiz.why: Two laying queens build a huge population for bigger honey crops, kept apart by management.

quiz.q: If you find eggs but cannot find the queen, a good rule is…
opts: Eggs mean she was there in the last ~3 days — the colony is likely queenright
quiz.why: Fresh eggs are strong evidence of a recent laying queen even if you can't spot her.

quiz.q: The scientific name of the Italian honey bee subspecies is…
quiz.why: Italians are A. m. ligustica — prolific, light-colored, popular in the US.

quiz.q: Carniolan bees (Apis mellifera carnica) are especially known for…
quiz.why: Carniolans are docile, overwinter well and build up fast in spring.

quiz.q: VSH (Varroa Sensitive Hygiene) is a trait where bees…
quiz.why: VSH bees interrupt mite reproduction by uncapping and removing infested brood.

quiz.q: Africanized honey bees are best described as…
opts: A defensive hybrid of African and European bees
quiz.why: They defend aggressively and abscond readily, but are the same species, A. mellifera.

quiz.q: 'Hygienic behavior' in bees refers to workers…
opts: Detecting and removing diseased or parasitized brood
quiz.why: Hygienic colonies uncap and remove sick brood, reducing disease spread.

quiz.q: Open mating means a queen's offspring genetics are…
opts: Uncertain — she mates with whatever drones are around
quiz.why: With open mating you can't guarantee the drones' stock.

quiz.q: A 'stock' of bees is…
opts: A group bred for a particular set of traits
quiz.why: Stock describes bees selected for traits like gentleness, honey, or mite resistance.

quiz.q: The standard movable-frame hive used across North America is the…
quiz.why: The Langstroth design exploits bee space so frames lift out without damage.

quiz.q: A queen excluder is used to…
opts: Keep the queen out of the honey supers
quiz.why: Its gaps let workers through but stop the larger queen from laying in honey supers.

quiz.q: Smoke calms bees mainly by…
opts: Masking alarm pheromone and prompting them to gorge on honey
quiz.why: Smoke disrupts alarm communication and makes bees eat honey, which calms them.

quiz.q: Honey is removed from frames WITHOUT destroying the comb using a(n)…
quiz.why: An extractor spins honey out by centrifugal force so comb can be reused.

quiz.q: Before extracting, beekeepers remove the wax cappings by…
opts: Uncapping with a knife, fork or roller
quiz.why: Cappings must come off so honey can spin out of the cells.

quiz.q: A screened bottom board helps with…
opts: Ventilation and monitoring Varroa mite drop
quiz.why: Mites that fall through the screen can be counted, and airflow improves.

quiz.q: An entrance reducer is mainly used to…
opts: Help a colony defend against robbing and cold
quiz.why: Shrinking the entrance helps weak or wintering colonies guard and stay warm.

quiz.q: Medium ('honey super') boxes are often preferred over deeps for honey because…
opts: They're lighter to lift when full
quiz.why: A full deep of honey is very heavy; mediums are easier on the back.

quiz.q: Comb foundation in a frame serves to…
opts: Guide bees to build straight, worker-sized comb
quiz.why: Foundation gives bees a template for even comb on the frame.

quiz.q: A nuc (nucleus) box is typically used to…
opts: House a small starter colony of a few frames
quiz.why: Nucs hold 2–5 frames to start or bank a small colony.

quiz.q: To keep mice out of a hive over winter, beekeepers commonly use…
opts: An entrance reducer or mouse guard
quiz.why: Mice nest in winter clusters; a small entrance or metal guard blocks them.

quiz.q: The most effective protection against bears at an apiary is…
quiz.why: Bears destroy hives for brood and honey; strong electric fencing is the standard defense.

quiz.q: A robbing screen works by…
opts: Forcing resident bees to learn a detour while confusing robbers
quiz.why: It reroutes the entrance so robbers can't find a straight way in.

quiz.q: Yellowjackets are most likely to attack honey bee colonies in…
quiz.why: Yellowjacket pressure peaks August–October as their own food runs short.

quiz.q: A 'zombee' is a honey bee parasitized by a…
quiz.why: Phorid fly larvae cause erratic night-flying behavior, hence 'zombee.'

quiz.q: 'Mite bombs' refer to…
opts: Collapsing colonies whose mites spread to others via robbing
quiz.why: When a heavily-infested colony crashes, robbing bees carry its mites home.

quiz.q: A commonly cited Varroa action threshold is about…
quiz.why: Many guides suggest treating when mite loads pass ~3 per 100 bees, lower before winter.

quiz.q: Compared with an alcohol wash, a powdered-sugar (sugar) roll is…
opts: Non-lethal but can be less accurate
quiz.why: The sugar roll spares the sampled bees; alcohol washes tend to give higher, more reliable counts.

quiz.q: Drone-brood trapping controls Varroa by…
opts: Luring mites into drone comb that you then remove
quiz.why: Mites prefer drone brood; removing capped drone frames removes the mites in them.

quiz.q: It's especially important to knock down Varroa in late summer to…
opts: Protect the winter bees being raised then
quiz.why: Mites peak as the colony shrinks; healthy winter bees are essential for survival.

quiz.q: Oxalic acid 'vaporization' works by…
opts: Heating the acid so it sublimates and coats the bees
quiz.why: A vaporizer sublimates oxalic crystals into a vapor that settles on the bees.

quiz.q: Why should you remove Varroa treatment strips on schedule rather than leaving them in?
opts: Leaving them in promotes mite resistance
quiz.why: Lingering low-dose exposure helps mites develop resistance.

quiz.q: The LD50 of a pesticide is the dose that…
opts: Kills half of a test population
quiz.why: LD50 is the median lethal dose — a standard measure of acute toxicity.

quiz.q: Neonicotinoid insecticides harm bees by…
quiz.why: Neonics bind acetylcholine receptors, causing neurons to fire uncontrollably.

quiz.q: A 'systemic' pesticide is one that…
opts: Spreads throughout all parts of the treated plant
Stays only on the leaf surface
quiz.why: Systemics like neonics move into nectar and pollen, exposing bees.

quiz.q: 'Sublethal' pesticide effects on bees include…
quiz.why: Sublethal doses don't kill outright but harm behavior and colony health.

quiz.q: To reduce bee kills, pesticides should ideally be applied…
opts: In the evening when bees aren't foraging
At midday on a sunny bloom
quiz.why: Spraying when foragers are home and blooms are closed reduces exposure.

quiz.q: An insecticide is a pesticide that targets…
quiz.why: Herbicides target plants, fungicides target fungi, insecticides target insects.

quiz.q: 'Entombed pollen' (cells of pollen sealed over with propolis and wax) often contains…
quiz.why: Bees wall off contaminated pollen; it's been linked to high pesticide residues.

quiz.q: Bees collect water primarily to…
opts: Cool the hive and dilute stored food
quiz.why: Water is used for evaporative cooling and to thin honey for feeding brood.

quiz.q: Pollen provides bees with protein, fats, minerals and…
quiz.why: Pollen is the colony's complete protein/micronutrient source; nectar gives carbs.

quiz.q: Bees need the MOST protein when they are…
quiz.why: Brood rearing demands protein for nurse bees to make royal jelly.

quiz.q: Turning nectar into honey involves removing water and adding…
quiz.why: Enzymes break sucrose into simpler sugars while bees evaporate moisture.

quiz.q: A diverse mix of pollen colors in the hive generally indicates…
quiz.why: Varied pollen sources provide a fuller amino-acid profile.

quiz.q: In fall, beekeepers feed colonies to…
opts: Help them reach enough stored weight for winter
quiz.why: Fall feeding (often 2:1 syrup) tops up winter stores.

quiz.q: Honey should never be fed to children under 1 year old because of the risk of…
quiz.why: Honey can carry Clostridium botulinum spores dangerous to infants.

quiz.q: Honey resists spoiling mainly because of its…
opts: Low moisture, low pH and hydrogen peroxide
quiz.why: Few microbes survive honey's dryness, acidity and natural peroxide.

quiz.q: Monofloral honey (like clover or orange blossom) comes from…
quiz.why: Monofloral honeys are dominated by nectar from a single plant.

quiz.q: Producing beeswax is 'expensive' for bees because they must…
opts: Eat a lot of honey to secrete wax
quiz.why: Bees consume roughly several pounds of honey per pound of wax made.

quiz.q: Propolis benefits the colony largely through its…
quiz.why: Resin-based propolis seals the nest and helps fight pathogens.

quiz.q: The largest managed pollination event in the United States is for…
quiz.why: Each spring well over a million colonies are trucked in to pollinate California almonds.

quiz.q: Honey bees are estimated to help pollinate roughly what share of the food crops we eat?
quiz.why: Pollinators are tied to about a third of crop foods, a common teaching figure.

quiz.q: Two colonies are most commonly united using the…
quiz.why: A sheet of newspaper between boxes lets the two slowly merge scents without fighting.

quiz.q: Queens are often marked with a paint color that indicates…
quiz.why: An international color code (white, yellow, red, green, blue) tracks the queen's age.

quiz.q: When moving a hive a short distance, the rule of thumb is to move it…
opts: Less than 3 feet, or more than a couple miles
quiz.why: Foragers reorient if moved far; small in-place nudges avoid losing them.

quiz.q: During swarm season, strong colonies are best inspected about every…
quiz.why: Weekly checks catch swarm cells before the colony issues a swarm.

quiz.q: 'Hefting' a hive means…
opts: Lifting the back to judge how heavy (how much stores)
quiz.why: A quick lift estimates food reserves, especially heading into winter.

quiz.q: A common way to PREVENT swarming is to…
opts: Give the colony more room or make a split
quiz.why: Relieving congestion — adding space, supering, or splitting — reduces the swarm urge.

quiz.q: In the Northeast, a colony generally needs roughly how much stored honey to survive winter?
opts: None — they don't eat in winter
quiz.why: Northern winters demand large stores; ~60–90 lb is a common target.

quiz.q: Reversing brood boxes in early spring is done to…
opts: Give the upward-moving cluster room to expand
quiz.why: The cluster drifts up over winter; reversing puts empty comb above them to fill.

quiz.q: You should generally avoid opening a hive when it is…
quiz.why: Bad weather chills brood and makes bees defensive; pick mild, calm days.

quiz.q: A quick first step when you find a colony has swarmed is to…
opts: Check that a viable queen cell or new queen remains
quiz.why: After a swarm, confirm the colony can requeen itself from a remaining cell.

quiz.q: American foulbrood is so hard to eradicate because its spores…
opts: Survive for decades and resist heat and chemicals
quiz.why: AFB spores persist in equipment for 50+ years, which is why burning is often required.

quiz.q: European foulbrood often improves when the colony…
opts: Hits a strong nectar flow or is requeened
quiz.why: Better nutrition and a brood break from requeening can let a colony recover from EFB.

quiz.q: Chalkbrood is most often triggered by…
quiz.why: The fungus takes hold in stressed, damp, or chilled brood; strong colonies resist it.

quiz.q: Nosema is best described as a disease of the adult bee's…
quiz.why: Nosema fungi infect the gut lining, harming digestion and shortening life.

quiz.q: Sacbrood gets its name because affected larvae…
quiz.why: The larva fills with fluid like a tiny water-filled bag, then darkens.

quiz.q: Deformed Wing Virus levels in a colony usually track with…
quiz.why: DWV is vectored by Varroa, so crumpled wings signal a serious mite problem.

quiz.q: Which is NOT a honey bee brood disease?
quiz.why: Nosema is an adult gut disease; AFB, EFB and chalkbrood affect brood.

quiz.q: A reliable way to tell AFB from EFB in the field is that AFB larvae…
opts: Rope out farther (2 cm+) and leave a hard, stuck scale
quiz.why: AFB ropes long and leaves a scale that won't lift; EFB usually kills larvae before capping.
